transcript.transcript: This is November 16, 2006, and this is an interview in Boise, Idaho, with Melissa Doddworth. Hey, Melissa, thank you so much for agreeing to do this interview about some of your memories about Julia Davis Park. Well, my husband, my then-husband, Alan Dobroth, and I came here from Portland. You had been hired as the director of the Boise Gallery of Art. And what year was that? He came in 69, and I came in early 70s. And of course, it was just the Boise Gallery of Art then, and it was still just the old WPA building. Very small. And Julia Davis Park had to be a focal point for us, because that's where he spent all his time, and I spent a lot of time. And I remember the park as being gorgeous. And it had still at that time the long alley of, and I forget what kind of trees, somebody might remember that, all the way from practically from Capitol Boulevard all the way back to the zoo. Which that got, I can't remember if they got diseased or what, but that was within the last maybe 20 years. And they all had to be taken out. And It's kind of replaced now, but it's much less formal alley than it was then. They were all uniform size, pretty much, and uniform growth. It was very, very elegant. And I never saw this, but I could imagine carriages driving down that lane, and also people equestrian, horse people riding horseback. It was easy to imagine that. I imagine that happened. And let's see, what else? The band shell, I think, was there in its old form. And I don't remember how that looked. But it seemed to be used almost, I would say almost every Sunday in the summer. And probably more really band concerts, that old, more old-fashioned kind of band concert, since having become more rock concerts and all that stuff. Yeah, well, at that time, of course, everybody's going to say this who remembers. Boise was much smaller, and things were much less scary. And sure, kids played in the park. I would say parental supervision, yes, but the parents were usually maybe pitching more shoes. There wasn't this tight kind of control on the kids. And I don't know. Well, then what? Ask a question. What else would be? Well tell me that you were here then when the WPA building for the art gallery was expanded. Yeah. The first expansion. Alan was the director. Do you know if they had to get permission to expand and to use from the Parks Department? As far as I understand it, the gallery building, now museum, isn't that good? Is still, I think, and was always, the property of the city. So permission in the sense, I think, only that the city itself had to not just approve, but be involved, of course, in any additional land being used to expand. I do remember vaguely that when the idea was being discussed there was talk of going up instead of out, sideways, possibly because of land use but I'm not sure about that. One of the problems I remember with the building as it existed was that there was a basement, and that that was the only place where they could house any kind of permanent collection. And there was a kind of a hodgepodge kind of permanent collection. It hadn't been curated in any way or anything. And the feeling was that that was an accident waiting to happen, a disaster waiting to happen, because of the water table being so close to the river. And I think it did even leak sometimes, and so they were very frightened of that. They may have not, you know, they didn't take the basement out of there, but I don't think it's used at all anymore. I'm not sure that Sandy would know. Sandy Levy. Yeah, I don't even know if it's, I mean, it may be completely closed off. Because that was one of the main things to expand the building. One of the main reasons was to get some storage on the ground level, and that also had to do with going up a floor even more floodproof, you know, to take it up, But that didn't happen. I don't know if that had to do with building bearing weight or whatever it was. Brad Shaw, you probably know, was the architect on the first expansion, which was, I forget what year that was, 75, perhaps. And Brad, if you haven't talked to Brad, he would be a good person, I think, to talk to. He, I believe, was the chairman of the first, Not the first Art in the Park in my memory, but the first time it was actually called that. Officially adopted by the museum as a fundraiser and called Art in the Park. So that was in the? I think that was 64. Again, the museum announced the 50th anniversary of Art in the Park recently. I think that was two years ago. So I think that would have been... 2004? Right, I think that's right. It might have been 2003. Art in the Park, I might as well go off on that. When they announced that, I went, What? It's older than that. I really believed that, and I still do believe that. But apparently it wasn't called Art in the Park. And I think also it had not been taken on by the museum itself as its fundraiser. I think it had been an annual kind of art fair put on possibly by the Da Vinci Guild, which was the first auxiliary for the Venn Gallery. When we moved here there were a few members left of the Da Vinci Guild who were still doing a little bit of fundraising at, by then it was called Art in the Park, but both arts had become the bigger auxiliary at that point. And I honestly can't tell you if there are any Da Vinci Guild women still alive. But another person who you might wish to talk to is Jane Oppenheimer. About all this early history there. I believe Jane, At some point, maybe in her teenage years, there was a social scene around the gallery, around the bringing it here, getting it built here, and I believe starting this outdoor art demonstration in Salem. Grew out of a group of kind of socialite women, which is not a bad thing, or it didn't used to be. Around, kind of the center point of that was Cornelia Hart and her mother. And Cornelia and her mom, I understand, traveled extensively in Europe. I believe They were both painters. And picked up on the sort of salon art society idea and started it up here with other women, I believe Cornelia's mother's age. And then as the daughters got older they took it on. And Cornelia Hart is deceased? She is and I think Jane was much younger but then became part of it. It went on and it was called the Society for Women in Art. I don't know what it was called but it had a name and it would appear on the social pages. This is stuff I found out when I went, what? 50th anniversary? It goes back farther than that. So I started trying to research it and I went through Statesman microfiche, microfilm, whatever, at the library And it was like a big headache, because of course, there was no indexing or anything like that. And I did find a few things, including reference to this society, whatever it was called, and to Cornelia's mother, and Cornelia, and to the young Jane Oppenheimer. So I think she might be a big, good resource about this. My understanding was that, oh. That would have been maybe referring to her as Jane Fogg? Yeah. My understanding, and again, it's just an impression, was that although they got the project to be built here, specifically to be an art gallery, the war came along. And of course, during that time, not much of anything happened. And so when the war was over, and I don't know how long after, late 40s maybe, the group, which now I believe Cornelia was an adult, and she may have been married by now. I don't know. But they were still these women who painted or were interested in painting, decided it was time to raise some money for this building, for the collection, to put things on the walls, to maybe hire somebody to oversee it and stuff, and began this, what became Art in the Park, which was to go down there and set up their easels and do plein air painting as demonstration to attract people and sell their work and give the money to the gallery. That's my impression of how Art in the Park started. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Great. I called someone. It may have been Brad, told me where, do you remember Mary Jenkins? Yes. Yeah. I can't tell you where she is. But Brad may remember if it was Brad who told me, or it may have been Chuck Hummel, who you're talking to, I'm sure. I'm sure. Yeah. That she was still alive, and this was probably 2004, if that's the right 50th anniversary, quote, unquote. And I think it was Arizona. And he told me the town. And I just got her number out of information, and there she was. And she also was maybe even a little bigger than I am, but I told her what I thought, and she said, you know, I think you're right about that. I think that's what I remember, too. So that's as much as I know. So it's been going on largely? I think it's been going on since the late 40s, but as Art in the park and as something the museum itself runs, as opposed to an auxiliary running or whatever. Yeah. Now, do you remember the Rose Garden? Was it right there? All I remember is that the Rose Garden was there, and I loved it, and they remodeled it, and I still love it. And expanded it. That's all I really remember. The existing sculpture court back in the back was and I can't remember the configuration but that open fencing, iron fencing, was a project that Alan, he added that to the museum. But of course, it was closer in because that big, huge room in the back wasn't there. So he moved it, and moved it out, and did different things with the landscaping. That fence, if that is the original fence and not just a not a remake of it, the original fence was made, designed and made by John Powell. And John is in Boston and has his own website which I could get you to if you want it. If you want to email him and ask him what he remembers. I've got it. I think I've even got it bookmarked. Remind me. I'll see if I can find it and get you to it quickly. I'm pretty sure he made that original open sort of thing. And at those days, it was used as a, were there events out there? Yeah, some. I think, I'm trying to picture it in my mind, and I can't really. There was some sculpture out there, but I can't remember what pieces were there. And I don't know that any that are there now were there then. That's what I don't recall. You might want to tell us about the sculpture. I think it's still there. As you come in to the park, and it's the frames. And it was one of the first part of the project. It was downtown. You remember it downtown, do you not? Tell us a little bit about it. I don't really remember how that happened. That would be who commissioned that? The Arts Commission, I think, commissioned that. I remember that it was very controversial, that there were a lot of people that had things to do. Well, that's true. And I always could kind of understand why, because if you look at it, it doesn't look like anything. And the whole idea of it was to look through it. I have seen, and I'm trying to think who took those photos. Maybe You've seen them. Some photographer here took some absolutely wonderful photos of it, or through it, when it was still down on that corner by City Hall. And it absolutely showed, these photographs absolutely showed how it was meant to be taken in. Yeah, framing buildings around, some of which are gone, of course, still, or already. What's the building Starbucks is in? It used to be Ah Fong's. Yeah, it used to be something. Kind of the same idea, in a sense, of those little view things that, what's his name? David Daniel Douglas, whatever his name is, has down there. By the Grove Hotel. Yeah, around the Grove, right. The same sort of thing. Those are really much older. Yeah, it was a disappointment to me that people didn't like it, because I really liked it a lot. But I'm certainly glad it didn't just get carted off to where nobody can see it. It's really nice that it's there. It's there now. It's at the museum. I think that's great. Let's see. I'm trying to remember. Gerhard Bourbonus might be somebody who would have some memory of Some of the stuff done around England in the 70s. He's a landscape architect? Yeah. He did the, I don't think it's there anymore. He did the remodeled Birmingham paths, concrete paths, and a beautiful hand - , What do they call that when they don't set stones in the mortar? They just clear it. He's so skillful. He did a lot of that work, so he might remember stuff, too, around the museum when it was expanded the first time. Did a lot of that and would know what was replaced by what, I think, is what I'm trying to get at, in terms of the exterior, the grounds around the building and stuff. Because it's changed since. Do you have any memories of when the, is it an Ochi piece that's on the outside of the gallery on the south side? Don't do south with me. As you're standing to go into the museum, it would be to your right. John Kilmaster? No. Yeah, that's the one, Kilmaster. It's outside? It's on the outside of the NAML. It faces the park. It faces the park. It faces the bandshell. That's John Kilmaster's. And were you involved when that was put in? Yeah, very much. Alan, the gallery, commissioned that piece. And John, I believe, had been fooling around with enamel on metal, but had this idea for a large piece. He'd never done anything really big. You know, there's either one or two out front of the Special Events Center for the state also. Right, right. But they're smaller pieces. Right. And in order to do this piece, he had to find somewhere to do this extensive. It's in pieces, but they're big panels. The enamelling work was done by a company in Seattle that's business was enameled stoves, like wood-burning stoves. And Alan and I got to go and see them at work on this project. And Marilyn, I have experienced two things in my life quite so exciting as seeing these workmen, who were used to doing enamel stoves, get to work on an art project. They were so excited. It was infectious. It was wonderful to see. And it turned out beautifully, I think. And John is still alive and just had a show, or was part of a group show, where, I'm trying to think, Was it Hyde Park Gallery, maybe? Or it might have been, It doesn't sound likely, but still somehow I think it might have been a visual arts collective. And I think the person who would know how to get hold of John, If you wanted to talk to him, it would be Tarmal Watai. Tarmal? W-A-T-I-A. I think he's in touch with John. So that might work out. And of course, if it was at Hyde Park, you could just call Hyde Park and ask them. I'm not sure. And visual arts also would be the same thing you listed. That's Sam Stimford. It's the glorious owner and runner of that place. What a great space that is. Have you been in this one? Yeah, it's cool. Down in Grove, it's in the Linen District. No, I haven't. Oh. You should do it. Yeah. Yeah, it sounds like some marvelous. It's a marvelous piece. It's going to be wonderful when it gets developed. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Anyway, so that's that piece. The stuff that's in the courtyard, in the sculpture court, I don't think I know anything about any of those pieces. They were all new to me, not new, but cool. Were you involved with the gallery when it became the museum? Yeah, I was on the board when we decided it was going to be BAM. And in order to go from a gallery to a museum, it had to meet certain standards. Is that right? Right. Some sort of, I don't even know what they call that. I really don't, can't remember what that's called, but you're right. It had to be visited and inspected, as it were. Yeah, why don't you turn it off for me, and I'll give you a gift. OK, you've just given me two pins that you will let us have for the historical library, our museum, for our work here. One is from the Boise Art Association, Boise Gallery of Art. And the other one is after it became the Boise Art Museum. That was our marketing launch there. We launched the new name, BAM. BAM. BAM. With lightning. And I wish I could tell you who designed that. I should know, but I just love it. I loved it then. We all loved it. It was great. Yeah. Well, of course, the changing to BAM, as far as I can remember, was done under Dennis O'Leary, I think, not under Rick. I could be wrong about that. But again, that's stuff that Sandy will know. But I don't even know if they have these buttons still. I just sort of kept them. I thought they were fun. It's wonderful. Yeah. Yeah. I'm trying to see if I can think of any more impressions about the park, but I can't except that I loved it. And I, You know, I'm also remembering, maybe remembering, that there weren't really sidewalks like there are now. I don't think there were. I think the grass came, there might have been a curb, but I think the grass came pretty much up to the roadways. No green belt? Oh, no. No. No. No green belt, No. I don't think that was even a gleam in anyone's eye when we got here. In fact, I know it wasn't, because it was very soon after we moved here that I think it was Boise State students who launched the initial small campaign to start cleaning up the river and getting those big pieces of concrete with rebar in them and all that stuff out of it. Just below Barber, I think that's where they started. And I also remember our even talking to each other about, what is this funny town with this gorgeous river? And they've turned their back on it. Not understanding, of course, that it had been a working river, and it wasn't recreational. It had not been seen yet as a recreational resource or whatever. Uh-huh. Yeah. So yeah, no, there was certainly no green belt in the bike path and all that stuff. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. Yeah. In fact, to ride a bicycle in Boise, then, was to take your life in your hands. Which I did. When you would go to the gallery, or later to the museum, Did you notice the people that were using the park for other purposes rather than the zoo? Well, as I said, I think I mentioned earlier, I believe there was already horseshoe, whatever they call those places where you play horseshoes. I think there must have been the tennis court still where the tennis court is, but I don't know that I was aware of it, not being a tennis player. Of course, the zoo was much smaller. I'd say there was more open land, of course, than there is now because of expansion of the zoo and expansion of the museum and so on. No tour train? No tour train. No Black History Museum? No Black History Museum. I was trying to picture the end of the park earlier. I mean, when we were talking earlier, I was thinking, what was down at that other end? Oh, of course there were. I don't know if that's not still there. Is it The little paddle boats, the little concession? Is it there? Mmhmm OK. OK, that's good. But they don't have the, they used to have a merry-go-round. I mean, like a real merry-go-round with horses. I don't know. I don't know if I remember that, but it might have been. Yeah, but I'm not sure what year it was a concession. Yeah, I don't know. I don't know. I don't think the usage was that different. Although, Because there was more open land, there may have been more larger parties of picnickers and that kind of stuff. But I don't remember that quite. It was just lovely. It was just a lovely park, a lovely big park. And I always liked it much better than Ann Morrison, because Ann Morrison was so formal, formal planting. It looked very... See, I should know the difference between the English garden and the French garden, but I don't. But Julia Davis Park just looked more informal and kind of wild, not wild like weedy, but you know. More casual. Yeah, and Ann Morrison always looked sort of military to me. Yeah, I get just more of a natural look. Yeah, and I think Ann Morrison looks much better now than it did then. Things are getting old. That time it wasn't attractive to us at all. It never went to the heart of it. Well, the trees were about three feet high. Well, and they were spaced exactly, and the bushes were spaced exactly, and it was like, Yeah, right. I did regret that the Morrison Center didn't get built in Hamilton. That's off the subject. Of course, Arthur Hart, I think, arrived here. Arthur and Dee, I believe, had come to Boise not too long before we did. Is that true to your knowledge? I don't know. And it turned out that Arthur had been an artist, and Alan was an artist. And so that immediately cemented. They became very good friends for a while, and helped. I think it helped because the audiences were both, they would be encouraged to go to one. Each one would encourage them to go to the other. So it was sort of a mutual thing. Helen was also very concerned that the relationship between the gallery and the college that was then the state was not good. And he had been told this, I guess, by the board that hired him. And it disturbed him a lot. So he made a lot of efforts in that direction to kind of be a liaison, kind of get and got to be pretty good friends with Louis Peck, who was head of the art department then, and Some of the art faculty, George Roberts, and, oh gosh, what was that photographer's name? Anyway, several faculty members over there got on the bandwagon and decided this disconnect or rivalry or whatever it was about was silly. So that helped a lot. And when you talked about Arthur Hart, was he then at the historical museum? He was the historic museum director. And I don't think he was, I'm not, that's something I don't know, whether he was also director of the historic society. But Arthur would again be someone Arthur would need to contact and talk to about the park. Because they would remember that, I hope, maybe better than I do, having been there, I don't know, a few years before we got there, I think. And of course, the footbridge to the college wasn't there at all. If you wanted to go from the gallery to the college or vice versa, you had to go around. Had to go around. And the bridge that has been restored was kind of not in good shape. The Capitol Bridge. The Capitol Bridge, yeah. That looks great. I don't know if they did that. Did you know that there was a Ten Commandments there in the park? Vaguely. You did? Yeah. I mean, it was just there. I mean, I didn't care one way or another, and I still don't, frankly. I've talked to people who didn't even know it was there. Yeah, I believe that. It wasn't very prominent, for sure. You know, it seemed to me kind of like a tempest in a teapot, all this stuff. I just thought, well, for heaven's sake. It's an historic sculpture or something. I don't care. You can put it back or leave it out. I don't care. I don't care. No. Let's see. The reason I'm asking is the year that we're doing this interview, it was on a ballot for Boise citizens. Exactly. Had a vote on whether to keep it. And they voted to put it back, did they not? No, not to put it back. Oh, they voted not to put it back. Do you see how much I care? So they voted to keep it where it is, at St. Michael's Episcopal Church, right across from the Capitol building. So I was just curious, knowing it was there. Yeah, I know. I did know it was there. There was something else there, but I don't know. It's still there. Was another dedicated by somebody, the DARs or the Foreign Legion or something. Yeah, a little monument. Yeah, is it still there? I think so Those things are just sort of things that are in parks. Yeah Just there right yeah Well, it sounds like you have good happy memories of the park. Oh, absolutely. Absolutely. I still love it. I think it's beautiful, and I love that they restored that gateway thing, that archway. I think that was just, I was working for the library when they started that, And everybody was saying, what are they doing over there? None of us knew. You'd think at the library we would. And then here it came, and I think it's great. I really like it a lot. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. So. OK. You

lines.line: # Introduction and Background
> Melissa Doddworth introduces herself and talks about her move to Boise from Portland in the early 1970s and her role at the Boise Gallery of Art.
** introduction, Boise, Portland, Melissa Doddworth, Boise Gallery of Art **
keywords: ** introduction, Boise, Portland, Melissa Doddworth, Boise Gallery of Art **

lines.line: # Memories of Julia Davis Park

story.description: Recorded on November 16, 2006. Topic is Julia Davis Park.
